In GitHub Copilot CLI, you can now leverage a multi-model reflection loop as a reviewer.

Super helpful for catching issues early before they compound.

[!\[\]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/burkeholland?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-image)

[Burke Holland](https://www.linkedin.com/in/burkeholland?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-name)

5d

The folks at [GitHub](https://www.linkedin.com/company/github?trk=public_post_reshare-text) released a new feature for the Copilot CLI called "Rubber Duck". And they say it can pretty drastically improve model perf.

You automatically get a review from a model from a different AI family at key points in the agent lifecycle - where it matters most.

Read more including their benchmarks: [https://lnkd.in/exggTrNU](https://lnkd.in/exggTrNU?trk=public_post_reshare-text)

You can use it today just by toggling on /experimental. Here's how...

This is the kind of shift that changes how engineering leaders think about code quality. Moving the review loop earlier and making it multi-model turns what used to be a bottleneck into a continuous feedback system.

The compounding issue is what most teams underestimate. One missed edge case in review becomes three production incidents downstream. Catching it at the CLI level before it ever reaches a PR is where the real leverage sits.

Curious how teams are balancing speed with the discipline to actually use the reflection output rather than just accepting the first suggestion. That is where the leadership culture piece matters most.

Multi-model reflection loops as a built-in review mechanism is an elegant architectural move — using model diversity as a quality layer rather than just a feature flag. For engineering teams, the implications go beyond code review: this sets a template for how agentic workflows can self-audit before issues propagate downstream. The "Rubber Duck" naming is also clever framing for something that's actually quite technically sophisticated. Curious to see how teams start composing this with longer agentic chains.

This is a subtle but significant shift in how we think about code quality in an AI-assisted world. The idea of using model diversity as a built-in review mechanism mirrors something I have been observing in leadership development - the best decisions come from structured reflection, not just faster execution. Teams that build reflection loops into their workflow (whether human or AI-powered) consistently outperform those who just optimize for speed. The real question for engineering leaders now becomes: how do you build this reflective discipline into your team culture, not just your toolchain?

Multi-model reflection loops are a game-changer for reliability at scale. In mission-critical domains like healthcare, legal, and defense, the cost of a single hallucination is not a UX issue - it is a compliance failure or worse. At Veebase, we are building this same layered-verification architecture into AI that serves clinicians, attorneys, and defense teams where every output has real consequences. This is the direction the entire industry needs to move.

This is a game changer for agentic workflows. Using a complementary model family (like pairing Claude with GPT-5.4) to break the 'bias loop' of self-reflection is such a smart way to handle complex, multi-file tasks. Catching a logic error in the planning phase is 10x cheaper than debugging it after implementation!

- [Report this comment](/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fsatyanadella_in-github-copilot-cli-you-can-now-leverage-activity-7448514963196911616-EWNZ&trk=public_post_comment_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=COMMENT&_f=guest-reporting)

Efficiency at the command line level is a tactical gain, but it doesn't address the systemic risk of unmanaged code expansion. The real bottleneck in enterprise software delivery isn't the speed of writing terminal commands—it's the cognitive load of maintaining the automated output that the team didn't architect. And in the infrastructure migrations I have advised on, adding Copilot to the CLI often masks a deeper problem: the lack of a standardized governance layer for automated deployments. Whether this applies in every vertical is a different conversation. You are simply accelerating the rate at which you hit a wall if the decision architecture remains manual. The tool won't fix a broken deployment strategy,and it shouldn't be expected to. Velocity is not governance.

"マルチモデルでのレビューエージェント" Rubber Duck がGitHub Copilot CLIへ実装。かなり良さそうです！ [https://lnkd.in/gAZfCWyQ](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgAZfCWyQ&urlhash=dQ-E&trk=public_post-text)

GPT-5.4 と Claude Opus 4.6 は、得意領域が結構違う。なので、Opusで計画やコードを作り、GPTでレビューする などの役割分担が、一部で注目されてました。今回のアップデートで、そのプラクティスがエージェント機能になりました。

GitHub Copilot のマルチモデルである強みが活きる機能で、単体モデルファミリー前提のClaude Code や Codex CLI とは異なる体験だと思います。まずはExperimental Modeから提供します。非開発用途でも是非。

⸻

GitHub Copilot CLI now has Rubber Duck, and this looks genuinely promising.

As many people who use these models already know, GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.6 has its own strengths. That is exactly why the workflow of using multi-models, like Opus for planning/coding and GPT for review, has already been considered by many power users. This update seems to productize that best practice.

More importantly, it shows the real advantage of GitHub Copilot’s multi-model approach. This is a compelling capability that is hard to replicate in single-model-family experiences like Claude Code or Codex CLI.

[!\[\]()](https://in.linkedin.com/in/mdshahinkam?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Mohammed Shahin](https://in.linkedin.com/in/mdshahinkam?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)

2d

- [Report this post](/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fmdshahinkam_i-had-set-up-a-codereviewer-subagent-and-activity-7448575161647280128-6FuW&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

I had set up a CodeReviewer subagent, and I had set up a policy at the [agents.md](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http%3A%2F%2Fagents%2Emd&urlhash=fPR1&trk=public_post-text) file, which would invoke the CodeReviewer agent for code reviews after every few commits. But there was one problem with this approach. Both the coding agent and the reviewer agent were using the same model. So any blindspots the coding agent had had, it was very likely that the reviewer agent had the same blindspots. So I tweaked the instructions for the CodeReviewer agent. I made the reviewer agent adversarial so that it would counter the systematic blindspots of the coding agent. After this change, it was able to catch some issues that the coding agent had missed. But yeah, with this Rubber Duck feature which uses a different model, I expect this to supercharge the coding output.

- [Report this post](/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fanthonyglines_i-was-hoping-to-give-a-talk-this-summer-in-activity-7442265773978574848-0gG9&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

I was hoping to give a talk this summer in GR on RAiDD. But, it looks like they have already selected the speakers and I'm not in the list.

But, more importantly, neither is AI!

Which my talk was very much about.

That's very interesting to me. VERY!

We talk about AI here. A lot. Some of you who are reading this are experts on the subject.

But at work... in the wild... everyone is scared of it.

No one understand what it does or how to use it!

This morning we had a meeting to go over MPC servers and ways to provide guardrails on our solutions. But to start small.

I snickered a little. No one noticed.

I delivered a fully functional guardrail system 100% customized for our codebase. I did some crazy sh!7 with that too. I used AI to help build the application using AI. Then I added chatbots to help me understand what it was I was building and how to use it!

So, what's the holdup, folks?

If you're not using it, why not? (all reasons are valid)
